I heard as one in a dream that messages of condolence had been received from Queen Alexandra, King George and Queen Mary, and hosts of our friends in England and India. Did Sunity Devee harbor any secret ambitions or dreams that she deliberately chose never to discuss with anyone?

Given Sunity Devee's deep reverence for her father Keshub Chunder Sen's progressive Brahmo theology rejecting caste, how would she have responded if one of her own children had wished to marry outside their social station in a way that would have genuinely scandalized British colonial society—would her principles override her social position?

Sunity Devee emphasizes her mother's courage in following her father against family pressure, yet she herself married into the maharani system. At what point, if any, would she have considered her own compromises with tradition a betrayal of her parents' principles?

She describes the Brahmo religion as one of 'tolerance and charity' and universal brotherhood, yet she was born into and maintained aristocratic privilege. How did she reconcile the gap between her father's egalitarian theology and her own participation in hierarchical feudal structures?

If forced to choose between defending her father's religious legacy and protecting her children's inheritance and status within the British-Indian colonial hierarchy, which would have taken precedence, and would she have been honest about that choice?

Sunity Devee critiques narrow-mindedness caused by caste belief, yet her autobiography is structured around her identity as 'Maharani'—a title entirely dependent on hereditary hierarchy. Was she aware of this contradiction, and if so, how did she justify it?

She portrays her mother's decision to leave her family for principle as admirable, yet describes her own life primarily through her marriage and children. Did she ever question whether she had internalized the very domestic limitations her mother had supposedly transcended?

Given that her father's Brahmo movement explicitly rejected caste, how did Sunity Devee explain or justify to herself the rigid social hierarchies she maintained as Maharani, including presumably the treatment of servants and lower castes?

The text emphasizes her father's suffering for his beliefs. Did Sunity Devee ever experience genuine suffering for her own convictions, or did her privileged position allow her to adopt progressive values without personal cost—and would she have acknowledged this difference?

She describes being 'fortunate' in her parents and their values, yet her marriage appears to have been arranged within the aristocratic system. At what point did she recognize that her own life choices were constrained by the very systems her father had critiqued?

If Sunity Devee truly believed in the universal brotherhood her father preached, why does her autobiography focus almost exclusively on her own elite circles, and would she have been willing to examine what that omission reveals about the limits of her conviction?
